generated_comment: Ananya: Aaj Sharma ji ne mujhe side mein bula ke bola — 'Ananya ji, aap bahut achha kaam karti hain, but you know how it is, the HNI clients in our area, they just feel more comfortable discussing their portfolios with a male RM. It's not about performance, it's just... client preference.' Yaar, do saal maine Rahul ko haath pakad ke sikhaya — every product, every client interaction — aur ab woh Deputy Manager ho gaya aur main wahi hoon jahaan thi. Client preference. Matlab meri mehnat ki koi value hi nahin hai, bas yeh mera gender problem hai unke liye.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The branch manager's justification naturalizes gender bias as neutral 'client preference,' framing Ananya's gender as a legitimate professional liability rather than acknowledging structural discrimination; Ananya recounts it with frustrated resignation, encoding institutional misogyny through the polite, business-register rationalization of denying a woman advancement despite demonstrably superior competence and mentorship.
persona_text: You are Ananya. Age 34. A woman working as a deputy manager at a government bank in Lucknow. You are talking to your husband at home after coming back from a particularly difficult day. Your male junior colleague who you trained for two years just got promoted above you. Your branch manager told you informally that you are doing great but that the customers in your area respond better to male relationship managers for high value accounts. You are telling your husband what was said.
